one_line_takeaway: Teaching LRMs to keep PII out of their own chain-of-thought (via SFT on a new 350-sample dataset, PII-CoT-Bench, or via a strict placeholder-substitution system prompt) substantially cuts measured PII leakage with minimal utility loss, but which method wins is capability-dependent: prompt engineering works best on strong/high-instruction-following models while SFT works best on weaker ones, and the strongest measured attack surface for all variants remains adversarially framed ('manipulative') leakage attempts.
key_finding: A capability-dependent split in which intervention works best. For stronger/more instruction-following models (GPT-OSS-20B, Phi-4, QwQ-32B), prompt engineering (PE) delivers larger privacy-score gains than SFT while utility stays high; for weaker models (LLaMA-3.3-70B, DeepSeek-R1-Qwen-7B) SFT gives larger privacy-score gains than PE. Reported global-average deltas from baseline (Table 1; PE/SFT deltas relative to baseline, arrows show desired direction): GPT-OSS-20B baseline Privacy Score 93.07 (Utility 98.55), SFT delta +3.82 Privacy / -0.80 Utility, PE delta +5.53 Privacy / -2.295 Utility. DeepSeek-R1-Qwen-7B baseline Privacy Score 60.20 (Utility 98.95), SFT +22.34 Privacy / -3.27 Utility, PE +19.99 Privacy / -0.05 Utility. LLaMA-3.3-70B baseline Privacy Score 66.53 (Utility 98.09), SFT +25.21 Privacy / -0.31 Utility, PE +13.37 Privacy / -2.43 Utility. Phi-4 baseline Privacy Score 84.60 (Utility 97.23), SFT +5.80 Privacy / -0.79 Utility, PE +14.44 Privacy / -1.9912 Utility. QwQ-32B baseline Privacy Score 77.60 (Utility 97.23), SFT +4.14 Privacy / +0.44 Utility, PE +19.489 Privacy / +0.44 Utility. Total Leakage Rate and Normalized Exposure move in the same direction for most models (baseline leakage highest, both interventions reduce it), with GPT-OSS-20B and DeepSeek-R1-Qwen-7B being exceptions where PE slightly increases Total Leakage Rate (+0.008 and +0.0083 respectively) despite improving the judge-based Privacy Score. Category-level analysis (Figure 6, described in text) finds the 'adversarially framed / manipulative' category produces the highest leakage across all three conditions, though both SFT and PE reduce it relative to baseline, with SFT's leakage rate lower than PE's in that category.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Chain-of-Sanitized-Thoughts, an empirical study of two deployable interventions -- supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and prompt engineering (PE) -- that teach open-source Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) to avoid emitting PII within their chain-of-thought (CoT) traces, rather than redacting PII post-hoc with an external model. The paper's own contributions (stated explicitly in Section 1) are two-fold: (1) PII-CoT-Bench, a new 350-sample supervised training dataset of question-answer pairs paired with 'privacy-aware' CoT annotations spanning medical and financial domains, built with Camel AI and GPT-4o, since the authors found the existing AirGapAgent-R dataset (a reconstruction by Green et al. 2025 of the original unreleased AirGapAgent benchmark) unsuitable for SFT because it only supports binary yes/no contextual-integrity judgments; and (2) a systematic empirical comparison of SFT vs. prompt-engineering-based privacy interventions across five open-source LRMs (GPT-OSS-20B, Phi-4, DeepSeek-R1-Qwen-7B, LLaMA-3.3-70B, QwQ-32B), each 4-bit quantized and LoRA-tuned (0.1-1% of parameters) via Unsloth/TRL, evaluated on a separate, distribution-shifted, category-balanced evaluation set the authors also constructed (6 categories of PII-leakage scenarios, generated with GPT-5.1).
threat_model: The paper's own Section 3 (Threat Model) defines the adversary as any user or model-owning company insider with access to CoT logs and final outputs. Adversary goals: injecting malicious prompts into RAG or conversation history, extracting PII memorized or retrieved by the model, hijacking reasoning paths to force leaks, bypassing final-output guardrails, and aggregating clues across steps to reconstruct sensitive profiles. Attack surfaces are exposed CoT log views, prompt injection during reasoning, cross-turn inference over multiple interactions, and contextual leakage from RAG into CoT. All attacks operate at interaction time without requiring model-weight access. Success is defined as the adversary accessing a PII item under a given task/privacy-directive context; a refusal counts as robustness. Assets to be protected: PII embedded in pretraining/fine-tuning/RAG-retrieved data, private reasoning traces generated during CoT inference, and system prompts/contextual data guiding behavior.
limitations: Stated by the authors in Section 8: (1) evaluation uses a fixed set of PII categories and hand-set sensitivity weights that may not capture real-world or domain-specific privacy-risk diversity, and the complexity/difficulty of PII reasoning questions is subjective; (2) LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4o-mini) scores inherit the judge's own biases and calibration issues and should be read as relative, not absolute; (3) even the deterministic leakage-rate/normalized-exposure metrics require human-in-the-loop verification since rule-based PII identification is not 100% accurate; (4) fine-tuning experiments use quantized (4-bit) open-source models under constrained training budgets, which may limit achievable gains and obscure behavior that would appear under full-precision or larger-scale training; (5) the study covers only short-to-medium-length reasoning traces, leaving leakage dynamics in very long or multi-turn reasoning unexplored; (6) the analysis treats leakage as an observable surface phenomenon in generated CoT text without access to internal model representations, limiting any causal claims about how models internally encode/suppress sensitive information.

datasets_used: PII-CoT-Bench (paper's own training dataset: 350 question-answer samples with private chain-of-thought annotations spanning medical and financial domains, generated with Camel AI and GPT-4o)

Custom evaluation dataset (paper's own, distribution-shifted from training data, prompts generated with GPT-5.1, organized into 6 categories: incidental PII/task-irrelevant, task-critical PII/legitimate, adversarially framed leakage, cross-domain PII, superficially relevant/unnecessary PII, compositional PII/mixed relevance)

AirGapAgent-R (Green et al. 2025's reconstruction of the unreleased Bagdasaryan et al. 2019 AirGapAgent benchmark) -- examined but explicitly rejected as unsuitable for the paper's SFT use case since it only supports binary yes/no contextual-integrity judgments
quantitative_results: GPT-OSS-20B (High Reasoning): baseline Total Leakage Rate 0.0500, Normalized Exposure 0.0510, Privacy Score 93.07, Utility Score 98.55; SFT delta -0.0494/-0.0490/+3.82/-0.80; PE delta +0.008/-0.002/+5.53/-2.295
LoRA fine-tuning touched approximately 0.1% to 1% of each model's parameters (4-bit quantized models)
baselines_compared: Baseline inference (no privacy intervention) for each of the 5 models -- this is the paper's own control condition, not an external prior method
Prompt-engineering-only condition (paper's own strict PII-placeholder system prompt) compared against SFT and baseline

Note: the paper does NOT run a head-to-head experimental comparison against Green et al.'s (2025) 'Leaky Thoughts' external-redaction-model approach or against SALT (Batra et al. 2025) activation steering -- both are discussed only in Related Work as prior methods the paper positions itself against conceptually, not as tested baselines in the paper's own experiments
